summary:
The presence of palpable purpura indicates underlying small-vessel vasculitis, commonly linked to conditions like HSP or drug reactions. Giant cell arteritis mainly targets large arteries and does not typically cause a purpuric skin rash; thus it lacks this feature among listed options. 

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{C}}

The summary should be concise and directly address what was asked—what condition is NOT associated with palpable purpura—and why based solely on vascular involvement type. Do not include any extraneous details about diagnosis methods, treatment plans, prognosis, etc., unless explicitly requested by further questions.

Only output your final summarized response here:

The absence of palpable purpura in giant cell arteritis stems from its primary involvement of large blood vessels, unlike other causes where small-vessel inflammation leads to visible purple spots under pressure. This makes giant cell arteritis distinctively unrelated to palpable purpura despite being an inflammatory disorder affecting medium-to-large vessels.  

Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{C}}$ To ensure clarity for future reference when answering similar queries involving dermatological signs related to systemic diseases – particularly distinguishing between types of vasculitic processes based upon their anatomical site-specificity – we emphasize that only certain forms of vasculitis manifest through characteristic cutaneous findings including palpable purpuras due largely to localized endothelial injury within smaller dermal capillaries versus larger arterial walls affected elsewhere clinically.  
Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{C}}$
